question: Does max_features affect Random Forest generalization on the breast cancer dataset?
note: Authored simulation of a human think-aloud session; see README.md. All quoted numbers are from real executor runs.

steps.content: Feature subsampling decorrelates trees in the ensemble; some subsampling should help relative to none, but the sweet spot for a 30-feature dataset is unknown. Start from scikit-learn's default, sqrt(n_features).
RandomForest, n_estimators=200, max_depth=None, max_features='sqrt', everything else fixed so only this axis varies.
cv accuracy 0.9623, std 0.0139, validation 0.9415. Solid anchor point.
This is the default and gives a strong baseline; nothing to compare it to yet.
Check the other extreme: max_features=1.0, i.e. every tree sees every feature, basically plain bagging with no feature subsampling.
Same RF setup, max_features=1.0.
cv accuracy 0.9422, std 0.0204 - both mean and stability worse than sqrt.
Consistent with the decorrelation intuition: without feature subsampling the trees are more correlated, so both the average performance and the fold-to-fold stability suffer.
log2(n_features) is another common default (~5 features here, close to sqrt's ~5.5); check it before interpolating.
Same RF setup, max_features='log2'.
cv accuracy 0.9598, std 0.0148 - slightly behind sqrt on both mean and stability.
Fewer features per split looks better so far across three points; want to check whether the trend is monotonic or U-shaped.
Fill in the middle of the range to check monotonicity: max_features=0.5, halfway between the sqrt/log2 neighborhood and using all features.
Same RF setup, max_features=0.5.
cv accuracy 0.9498, std 0.0177 - already worse than sqrt and log2, variance climbing.
No evidence of a better optimum further out; the relationship looks monotonic rather than U-shaped in this direction.
Add two more points to confirm the trend: max_features=0.3 (just above the sqrt/log2 neighborhood) and max_features=0.7 (further toward using everything).
cv accuracy 0.9548, std 0.0129 - sits neatly between log2 and 0.5, as expected.
Consistent with a monotonic trend.
cv accuracy 0.9473, std 0.0201 - continues the downward trend toward 1.0, in both mean and stability.
Six points now line up along a consistent trend from sqrt/log2 down to 1.0.
One thing not yet checked: does going below sqrt keep helping, or is sqrt already close to a floor? Try max_features=0.2 (~6 features, same neighborhood as sqrt's ~5.5).
Same RF setup, max_features=0.2.
cv accuracy 0.9548, std 0.0129 - ties the 0.3 run exactly, does not beat sqrt.
Going below sqrt's neighborhood does not buy anything further; sqrt itself still edges it out slightly.

steps.final_recommendation: max_features='sqrt' (roughly 15-20% of the 30 features) gives the best mean cross-validated accuracy and the lowest variance across folds; accuracy degrades close to monotonically as max_features increases toward 1.0, which is also less stable. No evidence of a second, better optimum. Recommend the scikit-learn default, sqrt, for this dataset.
